steps.title: Identify the grammatical role the blank must fill
steps.content: The sentence is "In English, this phrase ______ roughly to 'it's an old story.'" The subject is "this phrase" (singular, third person), and the blank is the only verb in the clause — it must be the main (finite) verb. A finite verb is conjugated to agree with its subject and carry tense; non-finite forms (infinitives, participles, gerunds) cannot serve as a main verb on their own.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
Test each choice as the main verb of "this phrase ______ roughly to 'it's an old story'":
  • Choice A — to translate: "to translate" is an infinitive — a non-finite form that violates the rule that a main clause requires a finite verb. ✗
  • Choice B — translating: "translating" is a present participle or gerund — non-finite, so it cannot be the main verb of the clause without an auxiliary. ✗
  • Choice C — having translated: "having translated" is a perfect participle — non-finite, and it typically introduces an adverbial phrase, not a main clause predicate. ✗
  • Choice D — translates: "translates" is the third-person singular present tense — a finite form that agrees with the singular subject "this phrase" and can serve as the main verb. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
